abstract.para: These functions create modified geometries by changing type, structure or vertices.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Add a point to a LineString.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Adds a point to a LineString before the index position (using a 0-based index). If the position parameter is omitted or is -1 the point is appended to the end of the LineString.
Availability: 1.1.0
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Add a point to the end of a 3D line
Guarantee all lines in a table are closed by adding the start point of each line to the end of the line only for those that are not closed.
refsection.programlisting: UPDATE sometable SET geom = ST_AddPoint(geom, ST_StartPoint(geom)) FROM sometable WHERE ST_IsClosed(geom) = false;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Given a geometry collection, returns a multi-geometry containing only elements of a specified type.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Given a geometry collection, returns a homogeneous multi-geometry.
If the type is not specified, returns a multi-geometry containing only geometries of the highest dimension. So polygons are preferred over lines, which are preferred over points.

If the type is specified, returns a multi-geometry containing only that type. If there are no sub-geometries of the right type, an EMPTY geometry is returned. Only points, lines and polygons are supported. The type numbers are:

For atomic geometry inputs, the geometry is returned unchanged if the input type matches the requested type. Otherwise, the result is an EMPTY geometry of the specified type. If required, these can be converted to multi-geometries using .

warning.para: MultiPolygon results are not checked for validity. If the polygon components are adjacent or overlapping the result will be invalid. (For example, this can occur when applying this function to an result.) This situation can be checked with and repaired with .

refsection.para: Availability: 1.5.0

note.para: Prior to 1.5.3 this function returned atomic inputs unchanged, no matter type. In 1.5.3 non-matching single geometries returned a NULL result. In 2.0.0 non-matching single geometries return an EMPTY result of the requested type.

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Extract highest-dimension type:
Extract points (type 1 == POINT):
Extract lines (type 2 == LINESTRING):

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns the simplest representation of a geometry collection.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Given a geometry collection, returns the "simplest" representation of the contents.

listitem.para: Homogeneous (uniform) collections are returned as the appropriate multi-geometry.
Heterogeneous (mixed) collections are flattened into a single GeometryCollection.
Collections containing a single atomic element are returned as that element.
Atomic geometries are returned unchanged. If required, these can be converted to a multi-geometry using .

warning.para: This function does not ensure that the result is valid. In particular, a collection containing adjacent or overlapping Polygons will create an invalid MultiPolygon. This situation can be checked with and repaired with .

refsection.para: Availability: 2.0.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Single-element collection converted to an atomic geometry
Nested single-element collection converted to an atomic geometry:
Collection converted to a multi-geometry:
Nested heterogeneous collection flattened to a GeometryCollection:
Collection of Polygons converted to an (invalid) MultiPolygon:

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a geometry containing curves to a linear geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Converts a CIRCULAR STRING to regular LINESTRING or CURVEPOLYGON to POLYGON or MULTISURFACE to MULTIPOLYGON. Useful for outputting to devices that can't support CIRCULARSTRING geometry types

Converts a given geometry to a linear geometry. Each curved geometry or segment is converted into a linear approximation using the given `tolerance` and options (32 segments per quadrant and no options by default).

The 'tolerance_type' argument determines interpretation of the `tolerance` argument. It can take the following values:
0 (default): Tolerance is max segments per quadrant.
1: Tolerance is max-deviation of line from curve, in source units.
2: Tolerance is max-angle, in radians, between generating radii.

The 'flags' argument is a bitfield. 0 by default. Supported bits are:
1: Symmetric (orientation independent) output.
2: Retain angle, avoids reducing angles (segment lengths) when producing symmetric output. Has no effect when Symmetric flag is off.
Availability: 1.3.0
Enhanced: 2.4.0 added support for max-deviation and max-angle tolerance, and for symmetric output.
Enhanced: 3.0.0 implemented a minimum number of segments per linearized arc to prevent topological collapse.
&sfs_compliant;
&sqlmm_compliant; SQL-MM 3: 7.1.7
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: This example uses 3D input.
Use only 2 segments to approximate quarter circle.
Ensure the approximated line is no more than 20 units away from the original curve, and make the result direction-neutral.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Change start point of a closed LineString.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Changes the start/end point of a closed LineString to the given vertex point.
Availability: 3.2.0
&Z_support;
&M_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Make a closed line start at its 3rd vertex.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a version of a geometry with X and Y axis flipped.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a version of the given geometry with X and Y axis flipped. Useful for fixing geometries which contain coordinates expressed as latitude/longitude (Y,X).
Availability: 2.0.0
&curve_support;
&Z_support;
&M_support;
&P_support;
&T_support;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the geometries into a "2-dimensional mode".

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the geometries into a "2-dimensional mode" so that all output representations will only have the X and Y coordinates. This is useful for force OGC-compliant output (since OGC only specifies 2-D geometries).
Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces was introduced.
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_2D.
&curve_support;
&P_support;
&Z_support;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the geometries into XYZ mode. This is an alias for ST_Force3DZ.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the geometries into XYZ mode. This is an alias for ST_Force3DZ. If a geometry has no Z component, then a Zvalue Z coordinate is tacked on.
Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces was introduced.
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_3D.
Changed: 3.1.0. Added support for supplying a non-zero Z value.
&P_support;
&curve_support;
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Nothing happens to an already 3D geometry.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the geometries into XYZ mode.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the geometries into XYZ mode. If a geometry has no Z component, then a Zvalue Z coordinate is tacked on.
Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces was introduced.
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_3DZ.
Changed: 3.1.0. Added support for supplying a non-zero Z value.
&P_support;
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Nothing happens to an already 3D geometry.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the geometries into XYM mode.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the geometries into XYM mode. If a geometry has no M component, then a Mvalue M coordinate is tacked on. If it has a Z component, then Z is removed
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_3DM.
Changed: 3.1.0. Added support for supplying a non-zero M value.
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Nothing happens to an already 3D geometry.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the geometries into XYZM mode.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the geometries into XYZM mode. Zvalue and Mvalue is tacked on for missing Z and M dimensions, respectively.
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_4D.
Changed: 3.1.0. Added support for supplying non-zero Z and M values.
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Nothing happens to an already 3D geometry.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Convert the geometry into a GEOMETRYCOLLECTION.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Converts the geometry into a GEOMETRYCOLLECTION. This is useful for simplifying the WKB representation.
Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces was introduced.
Availability: 1.2.2, prior to 1.3.4 this function will crash with Curves. This is fixed in 1.3.4+
Changed: 2.1.0. Up to 2.0.x this was called ST_Force_Collection.
&P_support;
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: This example uses a PolyhedralSurface.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Upcast a geometry into its curved type, if applicable.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Turns a geometry into its curved representation, if applicable: lines become compoundcurves, multilines become multicurves polygons become curvepolygons multipolygons become multisurfaces. If the geometry input is already a curved representation returns back same as input.
Availability: 2.2.0
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Orients all exterior rings counter-clockwise and all interior rings clockwise.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces (Multi)Polygons to use a counter-clockwise orientation for their exterior ring, and a clockwise orientation for their interior rings. Non-polygonal geometries are returned unchanged.
Availability: 2.4.0
&Z_support;
&M_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Force a polygon and its hole to use counter-clockwise exterior rings and clockwise interior rings.
refsection.programlisting: WITH data AS ( SELECT 'POLYGON((0 0,0 4,4 4,4 0,0 0), (1 1,3 1,3 3,1 3,1 1))'::geometry AS geom ) SELECT geom AS input_polygon, ST_ForcePolygonCCW(geom) AS ccw_polygon FROM data;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Orients all exterior rings clockwise and all interior rings counter-clockwise.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces (Multi)Polygons to use a clockwise orientation for their exterior ring, and a counter-clockwise orientation for their interior rings. Non-polygonal geometries are returned unchanged.
Availability: 2.4.0
&Z_support;
&M_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Force a polygon and its hole to use clockwise exterior rings and counter-clockwise interior rings.
refsection.programlisting: WITH data AS ( SELECT 'POLYGON((0 0,4 0,4 4,0 4,0 0), (1 1,1 3,3 3,3 1,1 1))'::geometry AS geom ) SELECT geom AS input_polygon, ST_ForcePolygonCW(geom) AS cw_polygon FROM data;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force geometries to use SFS 1.1 or 1.2 geometry types.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces a geometry to use the geometry types defined by the requested version of the OGC Simple Features Specification. The default is SFS 1.1. Curved geometries are converted to linear representations, TRIANGLE is converted to POLYGON, and TIN and POLYHEDRALSURFACE are converted to GEOMETRYCOLLECTION values containing POLYGON elements.

If version begins with 1.2, curved geometries are still converted to linear representations, but TRIANGLE, TIN, and POLYHEDRALSURFACE are preserved. Other version values use the default SFS 1.1 behavior. Geometry collections are processed recursively, and the SRID and Z/M coordinates are preserved.
Availability: 2.1.0
&P_support;
&T_support;
&curve_support;
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Compare the geometry types produced for SFS 1.1 (the default) and SFS 1.2. Read each row from the input type in the direction of the result columns.
refsection.programlisting: WITH inputs(geom) AS (VALUES ('CIRCULARSTRING(0 0, 1 1, 2 0)'::geometry), ('TRIANGLE((0 0, 0 2, 2 0, 0 0))'::geometry), ('TIN(((0 0, 0 2, 2 0, 0 0)))'::geometry), ('POLYHEDRALSURFACE(((0 0, 0 2, 2 0, 0 0)))'::geometry) ) SELECT ST_GeometryType(geom) || ' →' AS input, ST_GeometryType(ST_ForceSFS(geom)) AS "SFS 1.1 (default)", ST_GeometryType(ST_ForceSFS(geom, '1.2')) AS "SFS 1.2" FROM inputs;
refsection.screen: input | SFS 1.1 (default) | SFS 1.2 ------------------------+-----------------------+---------------------- ST_CircularString → | ST_LineString | ST_LineString ST_Triangle → | ST_Polygon | ST_Triangle ST_Tin → | ST_GeometryCollection | ST_Tin ST_PolyhedralSurface → | ST_GeometryCollection | ST_PolyhedralSurface (4 rows)

refnamediv.refpurpose: Force the orientation of the vertices in a polygon to follow the Right-Hand-Rule.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Forces the orientation of the vertices in a polygon to follow a Right-Hand-Rule, in which the area that is bounded by the polygon is to the right of the boundary. In particular, the exterior ring is orientated in a clockwise direction and the interior rings in a counter-clockwise direction. This function is a synonym for

note.para: The above definition of the Right-Hand-Rule conflicts with definitions used in other contexts. To avoid confusion, it is recommended to use ST_ForcePolygonCW.

refsection.para: Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces was introduced.
&Z_support;
&P_support;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a line extended forwards and backwards by specified distances.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a line extended forwards and backwards by adding new start (and end) points at the given distance(s). A distance of zero does not add a point. Only non-negative distances are allowed. The direction(s) of the added point(s) is determined by the first (and last) two distinct points of the line. Duplicate points are ignored.
Availability: 3.4.0

refsection.title: Example: Extends a line 5 units forward and 6 units backward

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a linear geometry to a curved geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Converts plain LINESTRING/POLYGON to CIRCULAR STRINGs and Curved Polygons. Note much fewer points are needed to describe the curved equivalent.
The perQuadrant parameter sets the minimum number of linear segments per quadrant required to reconstruct an arc. Values must be at least 2. The default value is 2.

note.para: If the input LINESTRING/POLYGON is not curved enough to clearly represent a curve, the function will return the same input geometry.

refsection.para: Availability: 1.3.0
&Z_support;
&curve_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: This example converts a linear Polygon approximating a circle to a CurvePolygon.
This example converts a 3D linear ring to a CircularString while preserving Z.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Return the geometry as a MULTI* geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns the geometry as a MULTI* geometry collection. If the geometry is already a collection, it is returned unchanged.

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: A Polygon is converted to a single-element MultiPolygon. The coordinates are unchanged.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Return the geometry in its canonical form.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns the geometry in its normalized/canonical form. May reorder vertices in polygon rings, rings in a polygon, elements in a multi-geometry complex.
Mostly only useful for testing purposes (comparing expected and obtained results).
Availability: 2.3.0

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a point projected from a start point by a distance and bearing (azimuth).

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a point projected from a point along a geodesic using a given distance and azimuth (bearing). This is known as the direct geodesic problem.
The two-point version uses the path from the first to the second point to implicitly define the azimuth and uses the distance as before.
The distance is given in meters. Negative values are supported.
The azimuth (also known as heading or bearing) is given in radians. It is measured clockwise from true north.
Negative azimuth values and values greater than 2π (360 degrees) are supported.
Availability: 2.0.0
Enhanced: 2.4.0 Allow negative distance and non-normalized azimuth.
Enhanced: 3.4.0 Allow geometry arguments and two-point form omitting azimuth.

refsection.title: Example: Projected point at 100,000 meters and bearing 45 degrees
See Also
refsection.para: , , PostgreSQL function radians()

refnamediv.refpurpose: Sets least significant bits of coordinates to zero

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: ST_QuantizeCoordinates determines the number of bits (N) required to represent a coordinate value with a specified number of digits after the decimal point, and then sets all but the N most significant bits to zero. The resulting coordinate value will still round to the original value, but will have improved compressiblity. This can result in a significant disk usage reduction provided that the geometry column is using a compressible storage type. The function allows specification of a different number of digits after the decimal point in each dimension; unspecified dimensions are assumed to have the precision of the x dimension. Negative digits are interpreted to refer digits to the left of the decimal point, (i.e., prec_x=-2 will preserve coordinate values to the nearest 100.

The coordinates produced by ST_QuantizeCoordinates are independent of the geometry that contains those coordinates and the relative position of those coordinates within the geometry. As a result, existing topological relationships between geometries are unaffected by use of this function. The function may produce invalid geometry when it is called with a number of digits lower than the intrinsic precision of the geometry.
Availability: 2.5.0

refsection.title: Technical Background
refsection.para: PostGIS stores all coordinate values as double-precision floating point integers, which can reliably represent 15 significant digits. However, PostGIS may be used to manage data that intrinsically has fewer than 15 significant digits. An example is TIGER data, which is provided as geographic coordinates with six digits of precision after the decimal point (thus requiring only nine significant digits of longitude and eight significant digits of latitude.)

When 15 significant digits are available, there are many possible representations of a number with 9 significant digits. A double precision floating point number uses 52 explicit bits to represent the significand (mantissa) of the coordinate. Only 30 bits are needed to represent a mantissa with 9 significant digits, leaving 22 insignificant bits; we can set their value to anything we like and still end up with a number that rounds to our input value. For example, the value 100.123456 can be represented by the floating point numbers closest to 100.123456000000, 100.123456000001, and 100.123456432199. All are equally valid, in that ST_AsText(geom, 6) will return the same result with any of these inputs. As we can set these bits to any value, ST_QuantizeCoordinates sets the 22 insignificant bits to zero. For a long coordinate sequence this creates a pattern of blocks of consecutive zeros that is compressed by PostgreSQL more efficiently.

note.para: Only the on-disk size of the geometry is potentially affected by ST_QuantizeCoordinates. , which reports the in-memory usage of the geometry, will return the the same value regardless of the disk space used by a geometry.

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.programlisting: WITH test AS (SELECT 'POINT (123.456789123456 123.456789123456)'::geometry AS geom) SELECT digits, encode(ST_QuantizeCoordinates(geom, digits), 'hex'), ST_QuantizeCoordinates(geom, digits) FROM test, generate_series(15, -15, -1) AS digits;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Remove a point from a linestring.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Removes a point from a LineString, given its index (0-based). Useful for turning a closed line (ring) into an open linestring.
Enhanced: 3.2.0
Availability: 1.1.0
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Guarantees no lines are closed by removing the end point of closed lines (rings). Assumes geom is of type LINESTRING
refsection.programlisting: UPDATE sometable SET geom = ST_RemovePoint(geom, ST_NPoints(geom) - 1) FROM sometable WHERE ST_IsClosed(geom);

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a version of a geometry with duplicate points removed.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a version of the given geometry with duplicate consecutive points removed. The function processes only (Multi)LineStrings, (Multi)Polygons and MultiPoints but it can be called with any kind of geometry. Elements of GeometryCollections are processed individually. The endpoints of LineStrings are preserved.
If a non-zero tolerance parameter is provided, vertices within the tolerance distance of one another are considered to be duplicates. The distance is computed in 2D (XY plane).
Enhanced: 3.2.0
Availability: 2.2.0
&P_support;
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Example: Collection elements are processed individually.
Example: Repeated point removal with a distance tolerance.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Removes points that are irrelevant for rendering a specific rectangular view of a geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a without points being irrelevant for rendering the geometry within a given rectangular view.
This function can be used to quickly preprocess geometries that should be rendered only within certain bounds.
Only geometries of type (MULTI)POLYGON and (MULTI)LINESTRING are evaluated. Other geometries keep unchanged.

In contrast to ST_ClipByBox2D() this function
sorts out points without computing new intersection points which avoids rounding errors and usually increases performance,
returns a geometry with equal or similar point number,
leads to the same rendering result within the specified view, and
may introduce self-intersections which would make the resulting geometry invalid (see example below).

If cartesian_hint is set to true, the algorithm applies additional optimizations involving cartesian math to further reduce the resulting point number. Please note that using this option might introduce rendering artifacts if the resulting coordinates are projected into another (non-cartesian) coordinate system before rendering.

warning.para: For polygons, this function does currently not ensure that the result is valid. This situation can be checked with and repaired with .

refsection.para: Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: The input polygon has 55 points. The result retains 15 points, while rendering the same shape inside the view envelope.
Only linework affecting the view is retained; existing vertices are reused.
refsection.programlisting: WITH data(geom, view) AS (VALUES ( 'MULTILINESTRING((0 0,10 0,20 0,30 0),(0 15,5 15,10 15,15 15,20 15,25 15,30 15,40 15),(13 13,15 15,17 17))'::geometry, ST_MakeEnvelope(12, 12, 18, 18) )) SELECT geom AS input_geom, view AS input_view, ST_RemoveIrrelevantPointsForView(geom, view, true) AS result, view AS view FROM data;
refsection.para: A line with no segment relevant to the view becomes empty.
Removing vertices without computing intersections can introduce self-intersections in the result.
refsection.programlisting: WITH data(geom, view) AS (VALUES ( 'POLYGON((0 10,0 0,10 -10,30 0,25 20,10 -5,0 10))'::geometry, ST_MakeEnvelope(-5, 1, 20, 12) )) SELECT geom AS input_geom, view AS input_view, ST_RemoveIrrelevantPointsForView(geom, view, true) AS result, view AS view FROM data;
refsection.para: With the default cartesian_hint, the function removes only points known to be irrelevant to the view.
refsection.programlisting: WITH data(geom, view) AS (VALUES ( 'POLYGON((0 30,15 30,30 30,30 0,0 0,0 30))'::geometry, ST_MakeEnvelope(12, 12, 18, 18) )) SELECT geom AS input_geom, view AS input_view, ST_RemoveIrrelevantPointsForView(geom, view) AS result, view AS view FROM data;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Removes small parts (polygon rings or linestrings) of a geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a without small parts (exterior or interior polygon rings, or linestrings).
This function can be used as preprocessing step for creating simplified maps, e. g. to remove small islands or holes.
It evaluates only geometries of type (MULTI)POLYGON and (MULTI)LINESTRING. Other geometries remain unchanged.
If minSizeX is greater than 0, parts are sorted out if their width is smaller than minSizeX.
If minSizeY is greater than 0, parts are sorted out if their height is smaller than minSizeY.
Both minSizeX and minSizeY are measured in coordinate system units of the geometry.

For polygon types, evaluation is done separately for each ring which can lead to one of the following results:
the original geometry,
a POLYGON with all rings with less vertices,
a POLYGON with a reduced number of interior rings (having possibly less vertices),
a POLYGON EMPTY, or
a MULTIPOLYGON with a reduced number of polygons (having possibly less interior rings or vertices), or
a MULTIPOLYGON EMPTY.

For linestring types, evaluation is done for each linestring which can lead to one of the following results:
the original geometry,
a LINESTRING with a reduced number of vertices,
a LINESTRING EMPTY,
a MULTILINESTRING with a reduced number of linestrings (having possibly less vertices), or
a MULTILINESTRING EMPTY.
Availability: 3.5.0

refnamediv.refpurpose: Return the geometry with vertex order reversed.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Can be used on any geometry and reverses the order of the vertices.
Enhanced: 2.4.0 support for curves was introduced.
&Z_support;
&P_support;

refsection.title: Examples

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a modified geometry/geography having no segment longer than a given distance.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a modified geometry/geography having no segment longer than max_segment_length. Length is computed in 2D. Segments are always split into equal-length subsegments.

listitem.para: For geometry, the maximum length is in the units of the spatial reference system.
For geography, the maximum length is in meters. Distances are computed on the sphere. Added vertices are created along the spherical great-circle arcs defined by segment endpoints.

note.para: This only shortens long segments. It does not lengthen segments shorter than the maximum length.

warning.para: For inputs containing long segments, specifying a relatively short max_segment_length can cause a very large number of vertices to be added. This can happen unintentionally if the argument is specified accidentally as a number of segments, rather than a maximum length.

refsection.para: Availability: 1.2.2
Enhanced: 3.0.0 Segmentize geometry now produces equal-length subsegments
Enhanced: 2.3.0 Segmentize geography now produces equal-length subsegments
Enhanced: 2.1.0 support for geography was introduced.

Changed: 2.1.0 As a result of the introduction of geography support, the usage ST_Segmentize('LINESTRING(1 2, 3 4)', 0.5) causes an ambiguous function error. The input needs to be properly typed as a geometry or geography. Use ST_GeomFromText, ST_GeogFromText or a cast to the required type (e.g. ST_Segmentize('LINESTRING(1 2, 3 4)'::geometry, 0.5) )

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Segmentizing a line. Long segments are split evenly, and short segments are not split.
Segmentizing a polygon:
Segmentizing a geographic line, using a maximum segment length of 2000 kilometers. Vertices are added along the great-circle arc connecting the endpoints.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Replace point of a linestring with a given point.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Replace point N of linestring with given point. Index is 0-based.Negative index are counted backwards, so that -1 is last point. This is especially useful in triggers when trying to maintain relationship of joints when one vertex moves.
Availability: 1.1.0
Updated 2.3.0 : negative indexing
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Change first point in line string from -1 3 to -1 1.
Change last point in a line string (let's play with 3D linestring this time)
refsection.programlisting: SELECT ST_SetPoint(foo.geom, ST_NumPoints(foo.geom) - 1, 'POINT(-1 1 3)') FROM (SELECT 'LINESTRING(-1 2 3,-1 3 4,5 6 7)'::geometry As geom) As foo;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Shifts the longitude coordinates of a geometry between -180..180 and 0..360.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Reads every point/vertex in a geometry, and shifts its longitude coordinate from -180..0 to 180..360 and vice versa if between these ranges. This function is symmetrical so the result is a 0..360 representation of a -180..180 data and a -180..180 representation of a 0..360 data.

note.para: This is only useful for data with coordinates in longitude/latitude; e.g. SRID 4326 (WGS 84 geographic)

warning.para: Pre-1.3.4 bug prevented this from working for MULTIPOINT. 1.3.4+ works with MULTIPOINT as well.

refsection.para: &Z_support;
Enhanced: 2.0.0 support for Polyhedral surfaces and TIN was introduced.
NOTE: this function was renamed from "ST_Shift_Longitude" in 2.2.0
&P_support;
&T_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: This example performs a forward longitude shift on a single point.
This example performs a reverse longitude shift on a single point.
This example shows that LineStrings are shifted only where coordinates require it.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Wrap a geometry around an X value.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: This function splits the input geometries and then moves every resulting component falling on the right (for negative 'move') or on the left (for positive 'move') of given 'wrap' line in the direction specified by the 'move' parameter, finally re-unioning the pieces together.

note.para: This is useful to "recenter" long-lat input to have features of interest not spawned from one side to the other.

refsection.para: Availability: 2.3.0 requires GEOS
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Move all components whose bounding boxes fall completely to the left of x = 0 by +360.
Move all components whose bounding boxes fall completely to the left of x = -30 by +360.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Snap all points of the input geometry to a regular grid.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Variant 1,2,3: Snap all points of the input geometry to the grid defined by its origin and cell size. Remove consecutive points falling on the same cell, eventually returning NULL if output points are not enough to define a geometry of the given type. Collapsed geometries in a collection are stripped from it. Useful for reducing precision.

Variant 4: Introduced 1.1.0 - Snap all points of the input geometry to the grid defined by its origin (the second argument, must be a point) and cell sizes. Specify 0 as size for any dimension you don't want to snap to a grid.

note.para: The returned geometry might lose its simplicity (see ).

Before release 1.1.0 this function always returned a 2d geometry. Starting at 1.1.0 the returned geometry will have same dimensionality as the input one with higher dimension values untouched. Use the version taking a second geometry argument to define all grid dimensions.

refsection.para: Availability: 1.0.0RC1
Availability: 1.1.0 - Z and M support
&Z_support;

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Snap a geometry column to a precision grid of 10^-3.
Display how the same grid size rounds a LineString.
This example snaps a 4D geometry.
With a 4D geometry - the ST_SnapToGrid(geom,size) only touches x and y coords but keeps m and z the same.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Snap segments and vertices of input geometry to vertices of a reference geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Snaps the vertices and segments of a geometry to another Geometry's vertices. A snap distance tolerance is used to control where snapping is performed. The result geometry is the input geometry with the vertices snapped. If no snapping occurs then the input geometry is returned unchanged.
Snapping one geometry to another can improve robustness for overlay operations by eliminating nearly-coincident edges (which cause problems during noding and intersection calculation).

Too much snapping can result in invalid topology being created, so the number and location of snapped vertices is decided using heuristics to determine when it is safe to snap. This can result in some potential snaps being omitted, however.
Performed by the GEOS module.
Availability: 2.0.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: A MultiPolygon snapped to a LineString at 1.01 times their distance.
refsection.programlisting: SELECT ST_Snap(poly, line, ST_Distance(poly, line)*1.01) AS polysnapped FROM (SELECT ST_GeomFromText('MULTIPOLYGON( ((26 125,26 200,126 200,126 125,26 125 ), ( 51 150,101 150,76 175,51 150 )), (( 151 100,151 200,176 175,151 100 )))') As poly, ST_GeomFromText('LINESTRING (5 107,54 84,101 100)') As line ) As foo;
refsection.para: A MultiPolygon snapped to a LineString at 1.25 times their distance.
refsection.programlisting: SELECT ST_Snap(poly, line, ST_Distance(poly, line)*1.25) AS polysnapped FROM (SELECT ST_GeomFromText('MULTIPOLYGON( (( 26 125,26 200,126 200,126 125,26 125 ), ( 51 150,101 150,76 175,51 150 )), (( 151 100,151 200,176 175,151 100 )))') As poly, ST_GeomFromText('LINESTRING (5 107,54 84,101 100)') As line ) As foo;
refsection.para: The LineString snapped to the original MultiPolygon at 1.01 times their distance.
refsection.programlisting: SELECT ST_Snap(line, poly, ST_Distance(poly, line)*1.01) AS linesnapped FROM (SELECT ST_GeomFromText('MULTIPOLYGON( ((26 125,26 200,126 200,126 125,26 125), (51 150,101 150,76 175,51 150 )), ((151 100,151 200,176 175,151 100)))') As poly, ST_GeomFromText('LINESTRING (5 107,54 84,101 100)') As line ) As foo;
refsection.para: The LineString snapped to the original MultiPolygon at 1.25 times their distance.
refsection.programlisting: SELECT ST_Snap(line, poly, ST_Distance(poly, line)*1.25) AS linesnapped FROM (SELECT ST_GeomFromText('MULTIPOLYGON( (( 26 125,26 200,126 200,126 125,26 125 ), (51 150,101 150,76 175,51 150 )), ((151 100,151 200,176 175,151 100 )))') As poly, ST_GeomFromText('LINESTRING (5 107,54 84,101 100)') As line ) As foo;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns a version of the given geometry with given ordinate values swapped.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a version of the given geometry with given ordinates swapped.
The ords parameter is a 2-characters string naming the ordinates to swap. Valid names are: x,y,z and m.
Availability: 2.2.0
&curve_support;
&Z_support;
&M_support;
&P_support;
&T_support;

refsection.title: Example
refsection.para: This example scales the M value by 2.
